text: This video demonstrates an operation of robotic-assisted right haematolectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis. This is a seventy-eight-year-old female with histology-proven adenocarcinoma in the cecum.

This video demonstrates an operation of robotic-assisted right haematolectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis. This is a seventy-eight-year-old female with histology-proven adenocarcinoma in the cecum.
The video shows the placement of surgical ports for the robotic-assisted right hemicolectomy: an 8mm suprapubic port, a 5mm port in the right iliac fossa, and additional 12mm ports.
The video shows the placement of surgical ports for the robotic-assisted procedure: an 8mm port at the suprapubic region, a 5mm port at the right iliac fossa, and additional 12mm ports.

The surgeon performs an initial laparoscopic assessment with the patient positioned head tilt and right side up, exposing the duodenum. The ileocolic pedicle is isolated and controlled using a laparoscopic stapler. A medial to lateral dissection is carried out, providing a clear view of the duodenum.

The surgeon performs an initial laparoscopic assessment with the patient positioned head tilt and right side up, exposing the duodenum. The ileocolic pedicle is isolated and controlled using a laparoscopic stapler. A medial to lateral dissection is carried out, providing a clear view of the duodenum.
The surgeon uses a single fenestrated grasper connected to bipolar diathermy in the left hand, while the right hand operates a robotic vessel sealing device, both visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a single fenestrated grasper connected to bipolar diathermy in the left hand and a robotic vessel sealing device in the right hand, manipulating tissues within the surgical field.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the lateral sidewall, mobilizing the hepatic flexure. The site of transection in the transverse colon is identified after controlling the right branch of the midocolic vessels, and the robotic linear stapler is used to divide the tissue.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the lateral sidewall, mobilizing the hepatic flexure. The site of transection in the transverse colon is identified after controlling the right branch of the midocolic vessels. The robotic linear stapler is used to divide the tissue at the identified site.
The surgeon performs a transection in the terminal ileum, placing the transected bowel above the liver for removal at the end of the procedure.
The surgeon performs a transection in the terminal ileum, placing the transected bowel above the liver for removal at the end of the procedure.
The surgeon places a stay suture in preparation for the site-to-site anastomosis, securing the tissue to facilitate the upcoming connection between bowel segments.
The surgeon places a stay suture in preparation for the site-to-site anastomosis, securing the tissue to facilitate the upcoming connection between bowel segments.

A laparoscopic burdock clamp is applied to the proximal small bowel to prevent spillage of its contents. Incisions are made in the transverse colon and small bowel, followed by the insertion of a robotic linear stapler.

A laparoscopic burdock clamp is applied to the proximal small bowel to prevent spillage of its contents. Incisions are made in the transverse colon and small bowel, followed by the insertion of a robotic linear stapler.

The surgeon performs a two-layer anastomosis, with the inner layer sutured continuously and the outer layer sutured with interrupted stitches. The use of the endo wrist allows convenient suturing with both hands.
The surgeon performs a two-layer anastomosis, with the inner layer being continuous sutures and the outer layer consisting of interrupted sutures.
The surgeon uses the EndoWrist instrument to suture with both hands, demonstrating the convenience and dexterity provided by the tool.
The surgeon applies interrupted sutures to complete the second layer of the anastomosis and removes the laparoscopic buttock clamp from the surgical field.
The surgeon applies interrupted sutures to complete the second layer of the anastomosis and removes the laparoscopic burdock clamp from the proximal small bowel.
The surgeon removes the bowel through a slightly extended umbilical port using a wound protector. The video shows the abdomen immediately after the surgery, with the surgical site visible.
The surgeon removes the bowel through a slightly extended umbilical port using a wound protector, followed by a view of the abdomen immediately post-surgery.
